title: Getting started with dScoreTest
text: dScoreTest implements debiased (Neyman-orthogonalized) score test. Given fitted null model, asks: direction model’s score systematically non-zero? , model misspecified. test computed sample splitting: held-hunt sample, flexible auxiliary fit searches promising direction h(x) misspecification. independent test sample, score along direction evaluated standardized. orthogonalization step absorbs plug-bias estimating h finite sample, test statistic asymptotically standard normal null — without assuming parametric form alternative. p-value one-sided (power lives right tail).

title: Getting started with dScoreTest
text: gof_test() — fitted model well-specified, nonparametric alternative? compare_models() — nested alternative capture signal null model misses? (anova()-style comparison.) dispatch fitted object, methods lm, glm, mgcv::gam. return dScoreTest object supports print(), summary(), plot().

title: Getting started with dScoreTest
text: use small linear model vignette builds quickly. Take truth linear x1 x2: p-value large: linear model correctly specified, test reject. Now introduce quadratic effect linear model capture: p-value small: test detects E[y2 | x1, x2] linear x1.
code: set.seed(3) n <- 300 dat <- data.frame(x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n)) dat$y <- 1 + dat$x1 + dat$x2 + rnorm(n)  fit <- lm(y ~ x1 + x2, data = dat) gof_test(fit) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of (Intercept), x1, x2. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 300, two-way split: hunt = 150, debias & test = 150 #>  #> T = -1.3137, p-value = 0.905519 dat$y2 <- 1 + dat$x1 + dat$x1^2 + dat$x2 + rnorm(n) fit.mis <- lm(y2 ~ x1 + x2, data = dat) gof_test(fit.mis) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of (Intercept), x1, x2. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 300, two-way split: hunt = 150, debias & test = 150 #>  #> T = 6.7450, p-value = 7.64993e-12

title: Getting started with dScoreTest
text: compare_models() tests null model richer alternative contains . add quadratic term explicit column: alternative’s quadratic term captures signal, comparison rejects. (compare_models() refits models model frames, supply extra terms plain columns — e.g. x1sq — rather -formula transformations like poly(x1, 2) (x1^2).)
code: dat$x1sq <- dat$x1^2 fit.0 <- lm(y2 ~ x1 + x2,        data = dat)   # null (linear) fit.1 <- lm(y2 ~ x1 + x1sq + x2, data = dat)   # alternative (superset) compare_models(fit.0, fit.1) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of (Intercept), x1, x1sq, x2. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = glm, debias.method = standard) #> n = 300, two-way split: hunt = 150, debias & test = 150 #>  #> T = 5.3831, p-value = 3.65976e-08

title: Getting started with dScoreTest
text: search direction misspecification (“hunt”) three styles, passed via hunt.style: "optimal" (default) — asymptotically optimal direction. "wls" — simpler weighted-least-squares hunt; can less powerful. "vanilla" — basic hunt; fallback.

title: Getting started with dScoreTest
text: default hunt gof_test() uses regression forest (grf), makes genuinely nonparametric goodness--fit test. See Goodness fit model comparison GAMs article worked examples mgcv::gam, including diagnostics via plot().

title: Checking generalized additive models
text: simulate four-term additive truth mgcv::gamSim(eg = 1), \[y = f_0(x_0) + f_1(x_1) + f_2(x_2) + f_3(x_3) + \varepsilon,\] \(f_3 \equiv 0\) \(f_0, f_1, f_2\) nonlinear. covariates independent uniforms.
code: set.seed(42) dat <- gamSim(eg = 1, n = 400, dist = "normal", scale = 2, verbose = FALSE)

title: Checking generalized additive models
text: gof_test() checks functional form fitted model nonparametric alternative built regression forest. correctly specified additive smooth model rejected: Formally, tests regression function \(E[Y \mid X_0,X_1,X_2,X_3]\) additive function \(X_0,X_1,X_2,X_3\). contrast, specify regression function linear function \(X_0,X_1,X_2,X_3\), model rejected. fact, since \(f_3=0\) data-generating mechanism, additive regression model without s(x3) still well-specified. Note: gof_test used testing significance predictor, illustrated . Since model fitted x0 x1, tests \[ E[Y \mid X_0, X_1] = f_0(x_0) + f_1(x_1) \] opposed \[ E[Y \mid X_0, X_1, X_2, X_3] = f_0(x_0) + f_1(x_1), \] simply gof_test see predictors x_1 x_2 appear model’s formula (can see printout y ~ X, X consists x0, x1). ask whether one omitted covariates carries signal, use compare_models(), gives hunt access alternative’s covariates.
code: fit.smooth <- gam(y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x2) + s(x3), data = dat) gof_test(fit.smooth) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = 0.6274, p-value = 0.265214 fit.linear <- lm(y ~ x0 + x1 + x2 + x3, data = dat) gof_test(fit.linear) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of (Intercept), x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = 9.7238, p-value = 1.19302e-22 fit.drop3 <- gam(y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x2), data = dat) gof_test(fit.drop3)   # f3 = 0, still well-specified given (x0, x1, x2) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = -2.3486, p-value = 0.990578 fit.drop23 <- gam(y ~ s(x0) + s(x1), data=dat) gof_test(fit.drop23) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = -0.4014, p-value = 0.655919

title: Checking generalized additive models
text: compare_models() tests null model larger alternative model detects signal living alternative’s extra terms. Similar ANOVA, can used test significance one predictors. example, can test significance \(f_2\) fitting model without s(x2) comparing full model. p-value correctly measures significance predictors x2, x3.
code: fit.full <- gam(y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x2) + s(x3), data = dat) res <- compare_models(fit.drop23, fit.full) res #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = gam, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = 11.8263, p-value = 1.42765e-32

title: Checking generalized additive models
text: plot() dScoreTest object shows diagnostic panels. rejected test, hunted direction h lines residuals, L = resids * h clearly non-zero mean. Indeed, see large positive slope ‘resids vs hunt’ plot .  summary() adds raw-vs-debiased comparison statistic, can see much outer orthogonalization step moved :

title: Checking generalized additive models
text: three hunts available GAMs. optimal hunt default; WLS hunt simpler sometimes nearly powerful:
code: compare_models(fit.drop23, fit.full, hunt.style = "optimal") #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = gam, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = 10.0491, p-value = 4.63434e-24 compare_models(fit.drop23, fit.full, hunt.style = "wls") #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = wls, hunt.method = gam, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = 10.1919, p-value = 1.07797e-24

title: Checking generalized additive models
text: section, follow Wakefield (Bayesian Frequentist Regression Methods, §12) fit GAMs analyzing prostate cancer data Stamey et al. (1989) Tibshirani (1996). want use data model continuous outcome variable log.PSA, log prostate specific antigen (PSA), biomarker predict clinical stage cancer.  dataset also measures following covariates: log.can.vol: log cancer volume (cc), digitized tumor area times thickness. log.weight: log prostate weight (grams). age: age patient (years). log.BPH: log amount benign prostatic hyperplasia, noncancerous enlargement gland, area (cm²). svi: seminal vesicle invasion, 0/1 indicator whether cancer cells invaded seminal vesicle. log.cap.pen: log capsular penetration, linear extent (cm) cancer extending prostate capsule. gleason: Gleason score, measure tumor aggressiveness. two largest cancer areas graded 1–5 (5 aggressive) two grades summed. pgg45: percentage Gleason scores 4 5. particular, want model visualize log.PSA varies according log.can.vol log.weight, accounting covariates. fit GAM model penalized regression splines covariate, except factors svi gleason, introduced simple linear terms. particular, fit two models joint effect log cancer volume log weight: one additive (interaction) one adds tensor-product interaction . Writing bivariate part ti() main effects plus ti() interaction functional-ANOVA decomposition, interaction term captures interaction. two fitted surfaces visualized follows.
code: prostate <- read.table(   "https://hastie.su.domains/ElemStatLearn/datasets/prostate.data",   header = TRUE) prostate$train <- NULL colnames(prostate)[c(1, 2, 4, 6, 9)] <-   c("log.can.vol", "log.weight", "log.BPH", "log.cap.pen", "log.PSA") head(prostate) #>   log.can.vol log.weight age   log.BPH svi log.cap.pen gleason pgg45    log.PSA #> 1  -0.5798185   2.769459  50 -1.386294   0   -1.386294       6     0 -0.4307829 #> 2  -0.9942523   3.319626  58 -1.386294   0   -1.386294       6     0 -0.1625189 #> 3  -0.5108256   2.691243  74 -1.386294   0   -1.386294       7    20 -0.1625189 #> 4  -1.2039728   3.282789  58 -1.386294   0   -1.386294       6     0 -0.1625189 #> 5   0.7514161   3.432373  62 -1.386294   0   -1.386294       6     0  0.3715636 #> 6  -1.0498221   3.228826  50 -1.386294   0   -1.386294       6     0  0.7654678 with(prostate, hist(log.PSA, breaks=15)) fit.0 <- gam(log.PSA ~ ti(log.can.vol, k = 6) + ti(log.weight, k = 6) +                        s(age,         k = 7, bs = "cr") +                        s(log.BPH,     k = 7, bs = "cr") +                        s(log.cap.pen, k = 7, bs = "cr") +                        s(pgg45,       k = 7, bs = "cr") +                        svi + gleason,              data = prostate, method = "GCV.Cp")  fit.1 <- gam(log.PSA ~ ti(log.can.vol, k = 6) + ti(log.weight, k = 6) +                        ti(log.can.vol, log.weight, k = c(6, 6)) +   # interaction                        s(age,         k = 7, bs = "cr") +                        s(log.BPH,     k = 7, bs = "cr") +                        s(log.cap.pen, k = 7, bs = "cr") +                        s(pgg45,       k = 7, bs = "cr") +                        svi + gleason,              data = prostate, method = "GCV.Cp") par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) vis.gam(fit.0, view = c("log.weight", "log.can.vol"), theta = 35, phi = 25,         zlab = "fitted log.PSA", main = "without interaction") vis.gam(fit.1, view = c("log.weight", "log.can.vol"), theta = 35, phi = 25,         zlab = "fitted log.PSA", main = "with interaction")

title: Checking generalized additive models
text: testing interaction, check model interaction well-specified:  indication misspecification.
code: set.seed(42) gof.1 <- gof_test(fit.1) gof.1 #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of svi, gleason, log.can.vol, log.weight, age, log.BPH, log.cap.pen, pgg45. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 97, two-way split: hunt = 48, debias & test = 49 #>  #> T = -0.2882, p-value = 0.613421 plot(gof.1)

title: Checking generalized additive models
text: compare_models() tests additive null fit.0 interaction alternative fit.1: comparison, mgcv’s approximate significance test interaction term ti(log.can.vol,log.weight) borderline significant.
code: set.seed(42) compare_models(fit.0, fit.1) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of svi, gleason, log.can.vol, log.weight, age, log.BPH, log.cap.pen, pgg45. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = gam, debias.method = standard) #> n = 97, two-way split: hunt = 48, debias & test = 49 #>  #> T = -1.5025, p-value = 0.933513 summary(fit.1)$s.table #>                                 edf   Ref.df         F      p-value #> ti(log.can.vol)            1.703735 2.105484 15.045410 2.986785e-06 #> ti(log.weight)             1.000000 1.000000 11.902001 9.354714e-04 #> ti(log.can.vol,log.weight) 6.425780 8.669048  2.566460 1.553072e-02 #> s(age)                     3.408446 4.140520  2.469979 4.903984e-02 #> s(log.BPH)                 1.000000 1.000000  5.313203 2.400945e-02 #> s(log.cap.pen)             3.735876 4.332359  1.747032 1.315853e-01 #> s(pgg45)                   3.592971 4.312329  1.863861 9.226700e-02

title: Checking generalized additive models
text: 97 observations, single debiased score test sensitive random hunt/test split. Re-running gives spread p-values: practical remedy aggregate many splits heavy-tailed combination test, robust strong dependence splits (cf. harmonic-mean p-value Wilson, 2019): aggregated p-value seems agree mgcv’s result.

what: Debiased Score Tests for Goodness of Fit and Model Comparison
title: Debiased Score Tests for Goodness of Fit and Model Comparison
text: Debiased (Neyman-orthogonalized) score tests assessing whether semiparametric parametric regression model well-specified comparing nested models. test uses hunt--test strategy sample splitting: held-hunt sample, fits null model uses machine learning find direction null model’s score seems positive; independent test sample, assesses significance score hunted direction. test employs orthogonalization eliminate plug-bias estimating null model, yielding test statistic asymptotically standard normal null without requiring parametric form alternative. Methods provided glm, lm, mgcv::gam fits well detecting heterogeneous treatment effects.

title: Debiased Score Tests for Goodness of Fit and Model Comparison
text: can install package CRAN development version GitHub
code: install.packages("dScoreTest") # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("richardkwo/dScoreTest")

title: Debiased Score Tests for Goodness of Fit and Model Comparison
text: Two entry points, S3 generics dispatch fitted model: gof_test() — fitted model well-specified, nonparametric alternative? compare_models() — nested alternative capture signal null model misses? simulate four-term additive truth mgcv::gamSim(eg = 1), y = f0(x0) + f1(x1) + f2(x2) + f3(x3) + noise, f3 = 0 f0, f1, f2 non-linear.
code: library(dScoreTest) library(mgcv)  set.seed(42) dat <- gamSim(eg = 1, n = 400, dist = "normal", scale = 2, verbose = FALSE)

title: Debiased Score Tests for Goodness of Fit and Model Comparison
text: gof_test() checks functional form fitted model nonparametric alternative. well-specified non-linear additive model rejected, forcing model linear . Note gof_test sees covariates model’s formula, tests whether E[y | covariates] assumed form, whether covariates missing.
code: fit.gam <- gam(y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x2) + s(x3), data = dat) gof_test(fit.gam)   # well-specified: not rejected #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = 0.6274, p-value = 0.265214  fit.lm <- lm(y ~ x0 + x1 + x2 + x3, data = dat) gof_test(fit.lm)   # misspecified:  #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of (Intercept), x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = 9.7238, p-value = 1.19302e-22

title: Debiased Score Tests for Goodness of Fit and Model Comparison
text: compare_models() tests null model nested alternative, detects signal living alternative’s extra terms. null drops s(x2) (real, sharp effect), alternative includes . functions return dScoreTest object print(), summary(), plot() methods. hunt direction misspecification can use optimal (hunt.style = "optimal", default), weighted-least-squares ("wls"), vanilla ("vanilla") algorithm.
code: # null model: well-specified since f3 = 0 in DGM fit.gam.null <- gam(y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x2), data = dat) compare_models(fit.gam.null, fit.gam) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = gam, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = 0.9375, p-value = 0.174243  # null model: misspecified, missing f2 fit.gam.mis <- gam(y ~ s(x0) + s(x1), data = dat)   res <- compare_models(fit.gam.mis, fit.gam) res #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = gam, debias.method = standard) #> n = 400, two-way split: hunt = 200, debias & test = 200 #>  #> T = 9.5337, p-value = 7.58703e-22 plot(res)

title: Compare two fitted GAM models — compare_models.gam
text: fit.0 null model fitted mgcv::gam object. fit.1 alternative model fitted mgcv::gam object. Must supermodel fit.0: every predictor variable fit.0's formula must also appear fit.1's. fit.0 fit.1 must fit rows order. hunt.style Hunting algorithm following options. 'optimal': optimal hunting (default). See hunt_optimal. 'wls': simpler hunting using weighted least squares, can less powerful. See hunt_wls. trim.outlier.hunt TRUE (default), extreme values produced hunted function trimmed using Tukey's IQR rule. splits Numeric vector length 2 3 giving relative sizes sample splits; rescaled internally sum one. Default c(0.5, 0.5), splits data two halves hunt test respectively. Though typically unnecessary practice, one can also specify 3-way split hunt, debiasing test respectively. verbose Default FALSE; information printed set TRUE. ... Unused; present S3 generic/method consistency.

title: Compare two fitted GAM models — compare_models.gam
text: object class "dScoreTest": list whose key elements debiased test statistic t.stat one-sided p-value p.val (right tail standard normal), along test-set score residuals, hunted direction, call. print, summary plot methods.

title: Compare two fitted GAM models — compare_models.gam
text: Nesting checked predictor-variable name , basis span: two models predictors different smooth specifications (e.g. s(x, k = 5) vs s(x, k = 20)) pass check. test remains meaningful long fit.1's class contains relevant alternative directions. Factor predictors, offset() terms, weights arguments, multi-column responses supported.

title: Compare two fitted GAM models — compare_models.gam
code: set.seed(42)  dat <- mgcv::gamSim(eg=1, n=500, dist="normal", scale=1) #> Gu & Wahba 4 term additive model  dat <- dat[, 1:5]    # test fit.0 against fit.1: well-specified (f3 = 0) and should not be rejected  fit.0  <- mgcv::gam(y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x2), data = dat)  fit.1  <- mgcv::gam(y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x2) + s(x3), data = dat)  compare_models(fit.0, fit.1) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = gam, debias.method = standard) #> n = 500, two-way split: hunt = 250, debias & test = 250 #>  #> T = 0.2087, p-value = 0.417349  plot(compare_models(fit.0, fit.1))    anova(fit.0, fit.1) #> Analysis of Deviance Table #>  #> Model 1: y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x2) #> Model 2: y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x2) + s(x3) #>   Resid. Df Resid. Dev     Df Deviance      F Pr(>F) #> 1    481.96     476.24                               #> 2    476.00     466.58 5.9557   9.6527 1.6634 0.1287    # mis-specified model: drops f2 and should be rejected  fit.00 <- mgcv::gam(y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x3), data = dat)  compare_models(fit.00, fit.1) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = gam, debias.method = standard) #> n = 500, two-way split: hunt = 250, debias & test = 250 #>  #> T = 13.7484, p-value = 2.60309e-43  plot(compare_models(fit.00, fit.1))    compare_models(fit.00, fit.1, hunt.style="wls") #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of x0, x1, x2, x3. #> (hunt.style = wls, hunt.method = gam, debias.method = standard) #> n = 500, two-way split: hunt = 250, debias & test = 250 #>  #> T = 13.2812, p-value = 1.48754e-40  anova(fit.00, fit.1) #> Analysis of Deviance Table #>  #> Model 1: y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x3) #> Model 2: y ~ s(x0) + s(x1) + s(x2) + s(x3) #>   Resid. Df Resid. Dev     Df Deviance      F    Pr(>F)     #> 1    491.06     3974.4                                      #> 2    476.00      466.6 15.062   3507.8 239.02 < 2.2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

title: Debiased score test: goodness-of-fit test and model comparison — dScoreTest
text: Test whether semiparametric (e.g., GAM) parametric (e.g., glm) regression model well-specified. test debiased (Neyman-orthogonalized) score test computed via sample splitting: held-hunt sample, null model fit flexible ML algorithm used hunt direction null model's score seems positive; independent test sample, direction's score evaluated assess significance. test employs orthogonalization eliminate plug-bias estimating null model, resulting test statistic asymptotically standard normal null without requiring parametric form alternative.

title: Customized debiasing for hte_test_conditional() — debias_hte_conditional
text: hunt \(\hat{h}(t,z)\), can rewritten $$\hat{h}(t,z) = \hat{h}_0(z) + t \cdot \hat{h}_{\Delta}(z),$$ \(\hat{h}_{\Delta}(z) := \hat{h}(1,z) - \hat{h}(0,z)\). Let projection \(\hat{h}(t,z)\) onto null space $$m_{\hat{h}}(t,z) = m_0(z) + t \cdot m_{\Delta}(z_{S^c}).$$ function returns debiased hunt function \(\hat{h} - \hat{m}_{\hat{h}}\).

S=1:(ncol(X)-1), CATE must constant; S=NULL, CATE unrestricted. folds.crossfit integer number folds cross fitting using R-loss estimate CATE. 1, cross fitting used. randomized FALSE (default), propensity \(e(Z) =      \mathbb{E}[T \mid Z]\) estimated cross-fitted grf::probability_forest. code: set.seed(42)  dat <- mgcv::gamSim(eg=1, n=400, dist="normal", scale=2, verbose = FALSE)  dat.0 <- dat[,1:5]    # well-specified  fit.0 <- mgcv::gam(y~s(x0)+s(x1)+s(x2)+s(x3),data=dat.0)  test.0 <- gof_test(fit.0)  # f3=0, also well-specified  fit.1 <- mgcv::gam(y~s(x0)+s(x1)+s(x2),data=dat.0)  test.1 <- gof_test(fit.1)  plot(test.1)    # misspecified  dat.1 <- dat.0  dat.1$y <- dat.1$y * dat$f0   fit.2 <- mgcv::gam(y~s(x0)+s(x1)+s(x2)+s(x3), data=dat.1)  test.2 <- gof_test(fit.2)  plot(test.2)

title: Goodness-of-fit test for GLM — gof_test.glm
code: set.seed(42)  n <- 500  X <- matrix(rnorm(n * 3), nrow = n)  # log(E[y]) ~ X well-specified  y0 <- 5 * exp(X[,1] + X[,3]) + rnorm(n) * 3  fit.0 <- glm(y0 ~ X, family = gaussian(link = "log"), start=rep(1,4))  gof_test(fit.0) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of (Intercept), X1, X2, X3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 500, two-way split: hunt = 250, debias & test = 250 #>  #> T = -0.2426, p-value = 0.595837  # log(E[y]) ~ X misspecified  y1 <- y0 + exp(6 * cos(X[,1]/6)^2) / sqrt(n)  fit.1 <- glm(y1 ~ X, family = gaussian(link = "log"), start=rep(1,4))  gof_test(fit.1) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of (Intercept), X1, X2, X3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf, debias.method = standard) #> n = 500, two-way split: hunt = 250, debias & test = 250 #>  #> T = 11.2969, p-value = 6.79367e-30

title: Goodness-of-fit test for a linear model — gof_test.lm
code: set.seed(42)  n <- 500  X <- matrix(rnorm(n * 3), nrow = n)  X[,3] <- X[,3] + X[,1] + X[,2] / 2  y0 <- 1 + X %*% c(1,1,2) + rnorm(n)  # well-specified  fit.0 <- lm(y0 ~ X)  test.0 <- gof_test(fit.0)  plot(test.0)    y1 <- y0 + cos(X[,1])  # mis-specified  fit.1 <- lm(y1 ~ X)  test.1 <- gof_test(fit.1)  plot(test.1)

what: Test for detecting heterogeneity in the conditional treatment effect (CATE) — hte_test_conditional
title: Test for detecting heterogeneity in the conditional treatment effect (CATE) — hte_test_conditional
text: binary treatment \(T\), covariates \(Z\) outcome \(Y\), tests hypothesis $$H_0^c(S): \tau(Z) \text{ depends } Z \text{ } Z_{S^c},$$ \(S\) subset covariates \(\tau(Z)=\mathbb{E}[Y | T=1, Z] - \mathbb{E}[Y | T=0, Z]\) CATE. \(S\) full set, amounts testing \(\tau(Z)\) constant; \(S\) proper subset, amounts testing \(Z_S\) effect modification holding \(Z_{S^c}\) fixed.

title: Test for detecting heterogeneity in the conditional treatment effect (CATE) — hte_test_conditional
code: hte_test_conditional(   y,   Tr,   Z,   S = 1:ncol(Z),   hunt.style = "optimal",   folds.crossfit = 5,   trim.outlier.hunt = TRUE,   splits = c(0.5, 0.5),   arg.hunt_grf = list(honesty = FALSE, tune.parameters = "all"),   verbose = FALSE,   randomized = FALSE )

title: Test for detecting heterogeneity in the conditional treatment effect (CATE) — hte_test_conditional
text: y Numeric response vector length n. Tr Binary (0/1) treatment vector length n. Z Numeric covariate matrix dimension n x p. S subset 1:ncol(Z) giving covariates whose effect modification tested (none null, given rest). Default 1:ncol(Z) tests heterogeneity (constant CATE null); S = NULL leaves CATE unrestricted. hunt.style One "optimal" (default), "wls" "vanilla", selecting hunting algorithm dScoreTest. hunted alternative grf outcome model fitted separately treated control arms (T-learner). folds.crossfit Number cross-fitting folds passed fit_CATE estimating CATE. Default 5. trim.outlier.hunt, splits, verbose Passed dScoreTest; see details. arg.hunt_grf Arguments passed grf::regression_forest() hunting. randomized FALSE (default), propensity \(e(Z) = \mathbb{E}[T \mid Z]\) used fit_CATE debiasing step estimated grf::probability_forest. TRUE, T assumed randomized (independent Z), \(e(Z)\) taken constant mean(T), fitted upfront without cross-fitting.

title: Test for detecting heterogeneity in the conditional treatment effect (CATE) — hte_test_conditional
code: set.seed(1)  n <- 600  Z  <- matrix(rnorm(n * 3), n, 3)  Tr <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(Z[, 1]))  y  <- Z[, 2] + Z[, 3] + Tr * (1 + Z[, 1]) + rnorm(n)  # CATE varies with Z1  # \donttest{  # allow modification by Z1 (S = {2,3}): well-specified, should not reject  hte_test_conditional(y, Tr, Z, S = c(2, 3)) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of T, Z1, Z2, Z3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf.hte, debias.method = hte.conditional) #> n = 600, two-way split: hunt = 300, debias & test = 300 #>  #> T = 0.5666, p-value = 0.285481  # forbid all modification (constant CATE): misspecified, should reject  hte_test_conditional(y, Tr, Z, S = 1:3) #> Debiased score test:  #> y ~ X, with X consists of T, Z1, Z2, Z3. #> (hunt.style = optimal, hunt.method = grf.hte, debias.method = hte.conditional) #> n = 600, two-way split: hunt = 300, debias & test = 300 #>  #> T = 3.8425, p-value = 6.08863e-05  # }

title: Plot the score test — plot.dScoreTest
text: Diagonotic plots test: Histogram \(\{L_i\}\), \(L_i = resid_i \times h_i\). \(\{L_i\}\) index \(\), \(\) refers \(\)-th observation full dataset. \(\)'s test split drawn. mean drawn horizontal line. Extremes values null can result bad normal approximation. case, consider setting trim.outlier.hunt=TRUE. Residuals (negative scores) versus hunted signal. horizontal segment drawn pair raw hunted signal debiased hunted signal. debiased gets higher, colored red; otherwise colored green. regression line (blue) large positive slope indicates model misspecified. Normalized \(\{L_i\}\) drawn order.
